name: Licorice Extract
short_description: Licorice extract is a botanical root ingredient used in digestive, throat, adrenal-support, and traditional wellness formulas, with important blood-pressure safety considerations.
overview_subtitle: A traditional root extract with meaningful formulation and safety differences.
overview_content:
Licorice extract comes from the root of Glycyrrhiza glabra and related species. It is used in supplements, lozenges, teas, and digestive formulas because the root contains glycyrrhizin, flavonoids, and other plant compounds.

One of the most important label distinctions is regular licorice versus deglycyrrhizinated licorice, often called DGL. DGL products are designed to remove much of the glycyrrhizin, a compound that can affect blood pressure and potassium levels in sensitive users.

Licorice extract should be approached carefully by people with high blood pressure, heart disease, kidney disease, pregnancy, or medication use. It is not a replacement for medical care.
interesting_fact: DGL licorice and standard licorice extract are not the same. The difference matters because glycyrrhizin is linked to many of licorice's safety concerns.
how_it_works_subtitle: Licorice activity depends on glycyrrhizin content and extract type.
how_it_works_highlight_description: Standard licorice may retain glycyrrhizin, while DGL is used in formulas where lower glycyrrhizin exposure is preferred.
how_it_works_content:
Licorice root contains triterpenoid saponins, including glycyrrhizin, along with flavonoids and other compounds. These constituents explain why licorice appears in digestive comfort, throat-soothing, and traditional wellness formulas.

Glycyrrhizin can influence mineral balance and stress-hormone metabolism, which is why regular licorice extract requires more caution than many botanicals. DGL products are commonly used when brands want the digestive-positioning of licorice with reduced glycyrrhizin exposure.

Users should carefully read the label and avoid assuming that all licorice products have the same safety profile.
benefits_subtitle: Benefits and risks depend strongly on extract type.

benefits_json.title: Digestive Formula Support
benefits_json.description: DGL licorice is commonly used in digestive comfort formulas.

benefits_json.title: Throat-Soothing Use
benefits_json.description: Licorice appears in teas and lozenges positioned around throat comfort.

benefits_json.title: Traditional Herbal Use
benefits_json.description: Licorice root has a long history in herbal systems and combination botanical formulas.

benefits_json.title: Stress-Response Positioning
benefits_json.description: Some formulas include standard licorice in adrenal-support blends, although safety review is important.

benefits_json.title: Label Transparency Value
benefits_json.description: Licorice is an ingredient where extract type and glycyrrhizin disclosure are especially important.

benefits_json.title: Formulation Context
benefits_json.description: Licorice is often paired with marshmallow root, slippery elm, aloe, or other digestive botanicals.

dosage_subtitle: Dose depends on whether the product is standard extract or DGL.
typical_dose: Use label-directed servings only; DGL and standard licorice extracts are not interchangeable.
dosage: Follow the specific product label and avoid long-term use of standard licorice without professional guidance.
dosage_content:
Licorice extract dosage varies widely by format, glycyrrhizin content, and intended use. DGL chewables, capsules, teas, and standard extracts may all provide different active profiles.

Because standard licorice can affect blood pressure and potassium levels, people should avoid self-directed high-dose or long-term use unless supervised by a healthcare professional.
safety_subtitle: Licorice has more safety considerations than many common botanicals.

side_effects_json.title: Blood Pressure Concern
side_effects_json.description: Standard licorice may raise blood pressure in some people.

side_effects_json.title: Potassium Depletion
side_effects_json.description: Glycyrrhizin-containing licorice can contribute to low potassium levels.

side_effects_json.title: Fluid Retention
side_effects_json.description: Some users may experience swelling or fluid retention with standard licorice.

side_effects_json.title: Digestive or Headache Symptoms
side_effects_json.description: Nausea, stomach discomfort, or headache can occur in sensitive users.

drug_interactions_json: Avoid unsupervised use with blood pressure medication.
Use caution with diuretics, corticosteroids, digoxin, or medications affected by potassium levels.
Discuss use with a clinician if taking heart, kidney, or hormone-related medication.
Do not combine multiple licorice-containing products without reviewing total exposure.
who_should_avoid_json: People with high blood pressure, heart disease, kidney disease, or low potassium.
People who are pregnant or nursing unless specifically advised by a clinician.
People taking diuretics, digoxin, corticosteroids, or complex medication regimens.
People preparing for surgery without reviewing supplement use with their care team.
Children unless directed by a qualified healthcare professional.
faq_subtitle: Common questions about licorice extract, DGL, and safety.

faq_json.question: What is licorice extract used for?
faq_json.answer: Licorice extract is used in digestive, throat, traditional herbal, and adrenal-support formulas, depending on extract type.

faq_json.question: What is DGL licorice?
faq_json.answer: DGL stands for deglycyrrhizinated licorice, a form processed to reduce glycyrrhizin content.

faq_json.question: Why does glycyrrhizin matter?
faq_json.answer: Glycyrrhizin can affect blood pressure, potassium balance, and fluid retention in some users.

faq_json.question: Is licorice extract safe every day?
faq_json.answer: Standard licorice should not be used daily long-term without medical guidance. DGL may be better suited for some digestive formulas, but labels still matter.

faq_json.question: Can licorice raise blood pressure?
faq_json.answer: Yes, standard glycyrrhizin-containing licorice may raise blood pressure in susceptible individuals.

faq_json.question: Who should avoid licorice extract?
faq_json.answer: People with high blood pressure, heart disease, kidney disease, low potassium, pregnancy, or interacting medications should avoid unsupervised use.

faq_json.question: Is licorice extract the same as licorice candy?
faq_json.answer: No. Supplements use concentrated root preparations, while candy may contain flavoring and different amounts of licorice compounds.

faq_json.question: What should I compare on a licorice label?
faq_json.answer: Check whether it is DGL or standard extract, the serving size, glycyrrhizin disclosure, and other active ingredients.

faq_json.question: Can licorice replace acid reflux medication?
faq_json.answer: No. Licorice supplements should not replace medical evaluation or prescribed treatment.

faq_json.question: Can I combine licorice with other botanicals?
faq_json.answer: Combination formulas are common, but people with medication use or health conditions should review the full formula with a clinician.

sidebar_profile_content: A traditional root extract used in digestive and throat-support formulas, with important glycyrrhizin-related safety considerations.

sidebar_quick_facts_json.label: Typical Dose
sidebar_quick_facts_json.value: Label-directed; varies by DGL or standard extract

sidebar_at_a_glance_content: Licorice labels should clarify whether the product is DGL or standard extract because safety considerations differ.

safety_level: Use caution with blood pressure, potassium, kidney, heart, and medication concerns
